plainMeaning: I meditate, in the lotus of my heart, on Pārśvanātha who contains every mantra, grants success in all things, and drives away ghosts and evil spirits.
recitation: sarva-mantra-mayaṁ sarva-kārya-siddhi-karaṁ paraṁ | dhyāyāmi hṛdayāmbhoje bhūta-preta-praṇāśakam ||

wordByWord.gloss: made of / consisting of all mantras (accusative, qualifying the meditated Lord)
wordByWord.etymology: sarva ('all') + mantra ('sacred formula') + -maya (taddhita suffix 'consisting of, full of'); accusative singular -aṁ agreeing with the object of dhyāyāmi.
wordByWord.notes: Bahuvrīhi/tatpuruṣa describing the Lord as the totality of all bīja-mantras — the source-title of the whole hymn's claim that it is 'made of all mantras'.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. sarva ('whole, all'); here the first member of the compound sarva-kārya-siddhi-kara.
wordByWord.notes: Printed as a separate word in the source but syntactically bound to kārya-siddhi-karaṁ.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. gerundive of √kṛ ('to do'), 'that which is to be done'; second member of the compound.
Skt. √sidh ('to succeed') + -ti; third member of the compound.
wordByWord.notes: Glossed at sutra level; here bound into sarva-kārya-siddhi-kara, 'maker of success in all undertakings'.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. agent-form of √kṛ, '-kara' ('making, causing'); accusative singular -aṁ, head of the compound sarva-kārya-siddhi-karaṁ.
wordByWord.notes: Closes the compound: 'the maker of success in every task.'

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. para ('beyond, highest, supreme'); accusative singular agreeing with the meditated object.
wordByWord.notes: Stands alone as an epithet of the Lord/mantra, not part of the preceding compound.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. √dhyai ('to meditate, contemplate'), present indicative 1st person singular.
wordByWord.notes: The single finite verb of the verse; all the accusatives are its objects.

wordByWord.gloss: in the lotus of the heart (locative)
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. hṛdaya ('heart') + ambhoja ('lotus', lit. 'water-born': ambhas 'water' + -ja 'born'); locative singular -e ('in').
wordByWord.notes: Printed with anusvāra (hṛdayāṁbhoje); the locative names where the meditation is fixed — the heart-lotus seat of the mantra-king.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. past participle of √bhū ('to be, become'), 'a being'; here a class of malevolent spirit; first member of the compound.
wordByWord.notes: Defined at sutra level (preta / piśāca / bhūta).

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. pra + ita, past participle of √i ('to go'), 'one gone forth / deceased'; second member of the compound.
Skt. pra- (intensive prefix 'forth, away') + √naś ('to perish') + causative-agent -aka ('one who destroys'); accusative singular -am, head of bhūta-preta-praṇāśakam.
wordByWord.notes: Closes the verse: 'the destroyer of ghosts and pretas.' Final m is the closing accusative-anusvāra reading of the source.

literalTranslation: Made of all mantras, the maker of success in every task, supreme, the destroyer of ghosts and pretas — [Him] I meditate upon in the lotus of the heart.
idiomaticTranslation: In the lotus of my heart I meditate on the supreme Lord, who is woven of every mantra, who brings every undertaking to success, and who annihilates ghosts and wandering spirits.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This colophon verse gathers the whole hymn into a single act of meditation: four accusative epithets all hang on one verb, dhyāyāmi, 'I meditate.' Where v.12 stationed the Lord externally — served by the Pārśva-yakṣa, worshipped by his guardian deities — v.13 internalizes him: the object of worship now sits hṛdayāmbhoje, 'in the heart-lotus,' the very seat the meditation-method (vv.5–8) prepared. The phrase sarva-mantra-mayaṁ is the verse's boldest claim: the Lord is not merely invoked by mantras but is the substance of all of them — a fitting summation of a stotra that has deployed bīja after bīja. Two complementary fruits are then named, one worldly and one protective: sarva-kārya-siddhi-karaṁ, the giver of success in every task, and bhūta-preta-praṇāśakam, the destroyer of ghosts and departed spirits. paraṁ, 'supreme,' set apart from the compounds, lifts these powers above the merely magical: he is the highest object of contemplation. The verse thus restates, in compressed devotional shorthand, the hymn's twin promise — mundane accomplishment and freedom from spirit-affliction — and binds both to a single inner practice. It sets up the closing v.14, where the poet, meditating on this same heart-borne Lord, attains 'all siddhi' for certain. The structure is deliberately frame-like: the all-mantra Lord enters at the start of the line and the spirit-destroyer closes it, enclosing the meditator's heart between sovereignty and protection.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse expresses the Jain understanding of dhyāna directed not at an intervening god but at the Tīrthaṅkara as the perfected object of contemplation: meditative remembrance (smaraṇa) of Pārśva is itself the efficacious act. 'Sarva-mantra-maya' encodes the mantraśāstra premise that the Arhat's name/form is the source of all seed-syllables, so that meditating on him subsumes every individual mantra. The pairing of worldly siddhi with spirit-removal reflects the bhayahara (fear-dissolving) function of the protective Smaraṇas, while paraṁ keeps the orientation soteriological — the supreme object, not a transaction.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: A reciter uses this verse as the gathering-point of the whole stava: having chanted the bīja-mantras and the body-meditation, here one simply fixes attention in the heart-lotus and holds Pārśva there as supreme, success-giving, and protective — a single sustained image to carry the protective intent of the recitation into daily fear or undertaking.

crossReferences.text: v.12 (Pārśva served by the Pārśva-yakṣa, worshipped by Dharaṇendra and Padmāvatī) and v.14 (meditating on the Lord in the heart to attain all siddhi)
crossReferences.source: Śrī Jīrāpallī Pārśva Stava, this edition

crossReferences.text: v.5–v.8 (the navel-to-crown channel, eight-petalled lotus, and the mantra-king awakening 'embodied in the heart')
crossReferences.source: Śrī Jīrāpallī Pārśva Stava, this edition

crossReferences.text: Upasargahara Stotra and Namiūṇa Bhayahara Stotra (the shared bhayahara / spirit-removing theme among the Pārśva Smaraṇas)
crossReferences.source: Nava Smaraṇa nos. 4–5, Panch Pratikraman Sutra – Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha ed.)

sources.title: Monier-Williams Sanskrit-English Dictionary (Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon)
sources.consultedFor: Roots and senses of dhyai (dhyāyāmi), ambhoja, kāryasiddhikara, sarva-...-maya, and pra+naś (praṇāśaka).

sources.title: Jirawala Tirth — Wikipedia
sources.consultedFor: Identification of Jīrāpallī / Jīrāvalā as the Pārśvanātha tīrtha and its alternate names (Jiravalli, Jirikavalli).

sources.title: Parshvanatha — Wikipedia
sources.consultedFor: Pārśvanātha as the 23rd Tīrthaṅkara, the object of meditation in this hymn.

sources.title: Jirawala Tirth — The Jainsite
sources.consultedFor: Devotional/ritual remembrance (smaraṇa) tradition of Jīrāvalā Pārśvanātha and the 'Oṁ Hrīṁ Śrī Jīrāvalā Pārśvanāthāya namaḥ' invocation.

notes: The verse is grammatically transparent classical Sanskrit (one finite verb dhyāyāmi governing four accusative epithets), so the rendering is secure. Ambiguity of reference: the accusatives may be read as qualifying Pārśva himself or, equally validly, the personified mantra-rāja meditated in the heart — the two are deliberately fused in this hymn, so 'the Lord in his mantra-form' is given. Source orthography preserved verbatim: 'સર્વ' and 'કાર્ય સિદ્ધિ' are printed as separated words though compounded in sense; 'હૃદયાંભોજે' carries anusvāra (hṛdayāṁbhoje) for hṛdayāmbhoje; the daṇḍa '।' and the closing period '.' are the book's own punctuation. The enumeration here is two spirit-classes (bhūta, preta); the broader bhūta/preta/piśāca set noted in the preface is not all present in this verse.
